video.title: Atari Versus Nintendo: The Curse of the Rabbit | Fully Ramblomatic

captions.text: In the very first one of these I did back in the  yellow background, beady eyes, shitty management
era, I talked about ET and how Atari 2600's glut  of low-quality third-party games led the way for
the Western games industry in the '80s like an  incontinent Scottish deerhound leashed to the
nose cone of a paper airplane, but today I would  like to tell you the sequel to that sorry story,
which features a new hero, an awakening  of power, the twist return of a villain
previously thought dead, a redemption arc,  and—in short—everything a good sequel needs
besides a Bruce Campbell cameo. I said in the  first instalment that the collapse of Atari is
what paved the way for Nintendo to take over  the West, dominate the global games industry,
and make an entire generation of American youth  fixated on trying to look up Princess Peach's
skirt in Smash Bros., but Nintendo were mindful  of not letting history run in cycles. How, when
they brought their Nintendo Entertainment System  to America, could they avoid Atari's mistake of
letting shitty third-party developers flood the  market with cheap, rushed-out underpantage? Their
solution was what one might call a very early  form of video game DRM. In the way that giving
a piggyback ride to a man holding an inflatable  globe was a very early form of GPS navigation.
Every NES console had what was called a CIC chip,  or Checking Integrated Circuit. A corresponding
chip was put in the cartridge of all Nintendo's  approved games. If the one chip couldn't detect
the other chip and exchange a passcode, the  console would enter a continuous reset cycle.
Simple, really. Compared to modern digital  security, it's basically one step up from
hiring a chimp holding a finger on the end  of a stick and having it constantly press
the reset button until the user can show it  ID or a tasty banana, but it seemed to do the
job. Only games with the chips would actually  work, and Nintendo could ensure that only the
games they approved of had the chips, but  some argued that Nintendo had overcorrected
in their attempts to bypass the potholes of  Atari's mistakes and driven straight into
the hedgerow of autocracy. As well as charging  licensing fees, Nintendo demanded exclusivity;
that developers only make five games a year  and remove all violent and family-unfriendly
content. Not that the world wept for the lack of  an NES port of Custer's Revenge, but it was still
suppressing creativity. And I hate being put in a  position where I have to argue that Mario should
be allowed to punitively spank disobedient  Yoshis; it makes everyone think I'm a weirdo.
So publishers were immediately looking for ways  to CI Circumnavigate the CIC chip. The publisher
Color Dreams took to adding tech to their  cartridges that reportedly deactivated the chip
with a voltage spike, the equivalent of stunning  the chimpanzee with a TASER gun. But to my mind,
the sort of person who'll fucking electrocute the  bouncer to get into a nightclub is not the sort
of person I'd want to be sharing a dance floor  with in the first place. And Color Dreams' games
were all notoriously shitty, their only  noteworthy title being Bible Adventures,
which certainly wasn't noteworthy for being  a hot ticket among the youth of the day.
Your nightclub equally doesn't want someone  standing in the middle of the dance floor
quoting Bible verses and making the atmosphere  weird. Nintendo quickly started manufacturing
consoles with defenses against voltage spikes.  Color Dreams attempted to distribute information
on how to disable them manually, but apparently  not enough people wanted to go at their expensive
electronics with screwdriver and pliers  for the sake of fucking Bible Adventures,
which to my mind reflects a shocking lack  of commitment to Jesus. But I promised
this story featured a surprise twist villain  resurrection in the form of none other than Atari.
Having been saved from annihilation by a partial  merger with Namco, Atari Games beheld Nintendo's
success with envious eyes. "So, those Nintendolts  think the world is safe from third-party slop,
do they? Soon, those fools will quake in fear  again at the irrepressible shittiness of Atari!"
And they immediately set out to back-engineer the  CIC chip to create their own version of it. Until
a few moments later when they said, "Oh, I've  just remembered: we suck at this. Is there a way
to get the CIC code that's easier and preferably  a little bit more evil?" And what do you know,
there was. See, Nintendo copyrighted the CIC, but  it turns out you can't copyright a thing without
telling someone what it is, and the U.S. Copyright  Office had a record of the unlock code. So Atari
cunningly put on a false moustache, went to the  Copyright Office, and said, "Erm, hello. Nintendo
are suing me for copyright infringement—sotto  voce—or at least they soon will be, and I need to
see their CIC code to argue our case in court."  And would you believe that actually fucking
worked? The Copyright Office cheerfully handed  it over, and Atari began releasing unauthorized
NES games under the Tengen label in stylish,  bad-boy black cartridges fitted with bootleg
CIC chips that they had named the Rabbit chip  presumably because it was a right little fucker.
At the same time, probably with an attitude of  "Welp, we're in the diarrhea now, might as well
start drinking," Atari filed suit against Nintendo  for unfair competition. Whereupon Nintendo blinked
in confusion a couple of times and then sued Atari  straight back for unfair competition and patent
infringement. Whereupon Atari counter-counter sued  Nintendo for unfair competition, patent misuse,
and being a bunch of big, fat, meanie heads. At  the trial, the judges fairly and reasonably nodded
along as they listened to both sides' arguments,  then coughed and said, "Sorry, could you go back
to the part where you deliberately committed fraud  against the Copyright Office?" Yes, you might have
already guessed from how we're very emphatically  not currently playing the Atari Switch 2 that
Atari didn't do terribly well out of this. Turns  out, if you break into someone's house and steal
their car, you don't then get to complain about  how aggressively they locked their garage door.
So ever since the dawn, gaming history has  been one long, futile struggle to find the
happy middle ground between overly liberal  third-party slop admittance and overbearing,
soul-crushing exclusivity, but  there's a coda to this story. See,
Nintendo might have won the trial, but they hardly  emerged unbattered. The courts concluded that,
had Atari back-engineered the chip properly,  it would have counted as Fair Use; they just
poisoned the well with that whole crime business,  and Nintendo's vulnerabilities were exposed.
More lawsuits against Nintendo's business  practices followed, along with a full-on
investigation by the Fair Trade Commission  that led to Nintendo having to pay through
the Nintennose. Under pressure from the legal  system and unhappy partners, Nintendo were
ultimately forced to relax some of their stringent  exclusivity policies, especially when they started
losing developers to a little upstart nobody  console calling itself the Sega Genesis. Yes,
inadvertently, Atari's needling of Nintendo  had set in motion a pattern that would define
the games industry for generations. To quote  Yoda in the second Star Wars prequel, "Begun,
the console wars have." Well. To paraphrase.  Yoda said, "Begun, the Clone Wars have," and
Clone Wars are different to console wars even  though they use the same letters. You see,
a Clone War is a conflict where the aggressors  inflict terror and despair upon the populace
with generation after generation of soulless,  hacked-out copy-pastes of anything that shows
the slightest competence, whereas a console  war—oh, bugger, I mixed up my note cards.
